gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major epipolar-geometry elements are present as plausible 3D geometry, with only minor omissions/stylization such as missing camera bodies or frusta, so it fits score 4.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls for mouse rotation/zoom, but this does not count as a meaningful interaction. A visible “P motion” range slider updates the 3D point P and visibly moves the epipolar plane, rays, projected points, epipolar lines, axes, and labels. There are pointermove and click handlers with raycasting over many scene objects; hovering shows compact tooltips and clicking posts/opens explanatory popups for elements such as the epipolar plane, rays, image planes, epipoles, and projected points, which is pedagogically useful. A reset-view button has a click handler but is hidden with display:none, so it is not usable. Escape hides popups/tooltips, but this is only a utility interaction. The UI is very compact in the bottom-left corner and does not cover important geometry.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_reasoning: Two meaningful pedagogical interactions are functional—the P-motion slider and hover/click explanations—while there is no guided demo or third visible parameter control, so this fits score 4.
gpt-5.5.faithfulness_analysis: The rendered figure contains the main conceptual elements from the source—two cameras, image planes, a yellow epipolar plane, red rays/epipolar lines, P, p1/p2, and e1/e2—so the diagram is recognizable.
